COMP27112 · Introduction to Visual Computing · Lecture 5

Region Processing
Edges, Convolution & Noise

Complete visual revision notes for Chapter 5 — built from the lecture slides, the practice quiz, and past-paper questions. Region processing computes each output pixel from a neighbourhood of input pixels, not just one.

Simple edge detection Convolution & correlation Kernels & normalisation Composite & separable filters Noise & noise reduction Rank / median filters

00

Point vs Region processing

In earlier lectures we did point processing: an output pixel depended only on the single corresponding input pixel (e.g. brightness I'(x,y) = I(x,y) − 28). Region processing uses a small neighbourhood of pixels around (x,y) to produce each output pixel — this is what makes edge detection, blurring and sharpening possible.

Key idea

The first simple region operator subtracts a neighbour from the current pixel and takes the absolute value:

I'(x,y) = | I(x,y) − I(x+1,y) |Difference with the pixel to the RIGHT → responds to vertical edges
01

Simple edge detection

An edge is a significant, local, extended change in image intensity. We can find edges by differencing adjacent pixels — large differences mean a steep intensity change (an edge gradient).

Worked example — a black square on a grey background

Take a region that is mostly grey (value 200) with a 3×3 black object (value 0) in the middle. Applying the difference-with-right-neighbour operator zeroes out flat areas and leaves bright values where intensity jumps.

Edge-of-image problem

At the right-most column there is no x+1 pixel. Three common fixes: (1) assume the missing pixel is 0; (2) wrap around to the opposite side (risky — the two sides may be unrelated); (3) crop the outer column (image shrinks, and mixing different-sized images later causes trouble).

Horizontal edges

Swap the neighbour: subtract the pixel below instead of the one to the right. This responds to horizontal edges.

I'(x,y) = | I(x,y) − I(x,y+1) |Difference with the pixel BELOW → responds to horizontal edges
02

Combining horizontal & vertical edges

We usually want all the edges. Given a horizontal gradient image and a vertical gradient image, there are three standard ways to combine them.

1 · OR the binary edge images

I'(x,y) = IH(x,y) ∨ IV(x,y)If a pixel is set in EITHER the horizontal or vertical edge image, set it in the output.

2 · City-block (Manhattan) distance

Add the absolute values of the two gradients, then threshold. Like walking round a city block — you cannot cut diagonally through buildings.

I'(x,y) = | I(x,y) − I(x,y+1) | + | I(x,y) − I(x+1,y) |

3 · Euclidean distance (gradient magnitude)

Use Pythagoras on the two gradient components — the true magnitude of the gradient vector.

I'(x,y) = √( (I(x,y) − I(x,y+1))² + (I(x,y) − I(x+1,y))² )
Thresholding edges

A weak gradient (say a jump from 2→4) is probably noise; a strong gradient (50→100) is a real edge. Increasing the threshold removes more of the weaker edges, cleaning up noise speckle but also discarding faint genuine edges.

One-pass combined operator

Rather than make two images and merge them, do everything in a single loop per pixel:

I'(x,y) = threshold( |I(x,y) − I(x,y+1)| + |I(x,y) − I(x+1,y)| , T )
Looking ahead

This “weights laid over the neighbourhood” idea generalises directly into convolution, the next topic.

03

Convolution — the operation

Convolution slides a small matrix of weights — the kernel — over the image. At each pixel it multiplies every weight by the pixel beneath it, sums the products, and writes the result to the output pixel under the kernel’s centre.

I'(x,y) = W ⊗ I(x,y) = Σs=−a..a Σt=−b..b W(s,t) · I(x−s, y−t)⊗ is the convolution symbol (also written *). W is the filter kernel — a.k.a. filter template or filter mask.

Step by step

The four rules of convolution:

The recipe

① Place the kernel on the image at a pixel. ② Multiply each weight by the pixel value beneath it. ③ Add up all the products. ④ Put the result into the output pixel under the centre of the kernel. Then slide along to the next pixel and repeat.

04

Convolution vs correlation

The minus signs in I(x−s, y−t) mean true convolution flips the kernel diagonally (180° rotation). The closely-related operation correlation does not flip the kernel.

Exam-friendly fact

Most image-processing software (including OpenCV’s filter2D()) actually performs correlation but calls it convolution. It rarely matters because the kernels we use are usually symmetric — flipping a symmetric kernel changes nothing — and we can design our kernels to suit whichever operation is used.

05

Practical issues & normalisation

Can convolution be done in-place?

Answer: No

Point processing can write the result back into the same memory location, because each pixel is independent. Convolution cannot be done in-place: once you overwrite a pixel, the next kernel position would read the new (wrong) value, corrupting all later calculations. You need a separate output image.

Kernel shape

Kernels are normally square and odd-sized (3×3, 5×5, 7×7). An odd size guarantees a well-defined centre pixel to write the output into.

The edge-of-image problem (again)

Same options as before: treat off-image pixels as 0, wrap around, or shrink the image by cropping the border.

Don’t confuse “mask” meanings

Two kinds of “mask”

A filter mask = a convolution kernel. A binary mask contains only 0 and 255 and is AND-ed with an image to keep/remove regions (e.g. the balloons segmented by hue). Different concepts that share a word.

Normalisation

If the kernel weights don’t sum to 1, the result can overflow the 0–255 range. Two fixes:

  • Normalise the output: work in an integer image, sum the products, then divide by the total weight (÷9 here).
  • Normalise the kernel: divide every weight by 9 up-front, giving a floating-point kernel of 1/9 values that already keeps results in range.
Why normalise to sum = 1?

So a smoothing/averaging kernel preserves overall brightness and the answer does not overflow the unsigned-byte range.

06

Composite & separable kernels

Convolution is associative

A ⊗ (B ⊗ C) = (A ⊗ B) ⊗ CA, B are kernels; C is the image.

So instead of convolving an image twice with the same kernel, you can pre-combine the two kernels once and convolve the image a single time.

Trade-off

Applying the 3×3 twice = 2×9 = 18 multiply-adds per pixel. The composite 5×5 = 25 multiply-adds per pixel — actually more work here. Composition is a win when it lets you reuse one expensive combined kernel many times, or feeds into the separability trick below.

Separable kernels — the real efficiency win

Some 2-D kernels are the convolution of a row vector and a column vector. Such kernels are separable.

[1 2 1] ⊗ [1 2 1]ᵀ
121
242
121

Why separating helps — the arithmetic

3×3 directly: 9 multiply-adds per pixel → 9·M·N total.

Separated 1×3 then 3×1: 3 + 3 = 6 per pixel → 2·3·M·N = 6MN. That’s ⅔ of the work — a modest gain.

Exam question (slide 37): what about a 7×7 kernel?

• Direct: 7×7 = 49 MN multiply-adds. • Separated 1×7 then 7×1: 7 + 7 = 14 MN. The bigger the kernel, the bigger the saving (49 → 14).

Quiz fact

A square template is separable into two 1-D templates if the convolution of the two 1-D templates equals the original square template.

07

Applications of convolution

By changing the kernel values, one convolution routine performs many different effects. The same code, different weights.

Smoothing — the box filter

Sharpening

Sharpening kernel
0−10
−15−1
0−10
Note the weights

The sharpening kernel sums to 5 − 4 = 1, so it preserves brightness on flat areas while boosting edges. The big centre weight (5) sitting on a noisy pixel amplifies that noise.

Laplacian (edge detection by convolution)

Laplacian kernel
0−10
−14−1
0−10
Why it finds edges

The Laplacian weights sum to 4 − 4 = 0. Over a uniform area (all black or all white) the output is 0; only where intensity changes — an edge — does it produce a non-zero response. Like sharpening, it amplifies noise.

08

Noise

Noise is the deviation of a value from its expected value. (Quiz: noise is “any factor in the imaging system that gives rise to perturbations in the image”.)

Types of noise

TypeModelCharacteristics
Random / Gaussianx → x + nn can be ±; random distribution, zero mean; usually much smaller than max(x) (high signal-to-noise ratio).
Salt & pepperx → {max, min} randomlyPixels forced to 0 or 255; much less common; from spikes on a transmission channel.
Imaging artefacts—Streaks and blooms from the sensor/optics.
Quiz fact

Noise is generally assumed to be zero-mean, Gaussian-distributed.

Sources of noise — two schools of thought

Preferred definition: anything within the imaging system that causes a change — electrical interference, optical aberration. The broader view also counts external causes like atmospheric disturbance.

Examples of real-world noise

09

Noise reduction

The core principle

Σ(x + n) = Σ(x) + Σ(n) ≈ Σ(x)Because the noise n is random and zero-mean, summing many samples makes the noise cancel out, leaving the signal.

Three flavours of smoothing:

  • Local smoothing — convolve with a smoothing kernel. Reduces noise but removes detail, can introduce ringing, and the noise amplitude falls with template length.
  • Temporal smoothing — average multiple images of the same scene (“stacking” in astronomy). Often needs image registration first to align frames.

Adaptive smoothing

g' = s if |s − g| < T ; otherwise g' = gg = original pixel, s = smoothed value, T = threshold. Keeps edges sharp by only smoothing where the change is small.

Gaussian smoothing

Why Gaussian?

A box filter weights all neighbours equally and can cause ringing (overshoot, like an under-damped oscilloscope trace). A Gaussian kernel weights central pixels more heavily, which introduces fewer artefacts at object edges (the quiz answer for “why Gaussian is a good choice”).

10

Rank filters — the median filter

A rank filter moves a window over the image like convolution, but instead of a weighted sum it sorts the neighbourhood and picks a ranked value. The median filter picks the middle value.

Worked example (slide 66)

Neighbourhood values: 5 9 6 191 11 4 6 2 12

Sorted: 2 4 5 6 6 9 11 12 191

The median = 6 goes into the centre pixel. The likely-noise value 191 is pushed to the end of the sorted list and has no effect on the output.

Median vs averaging

The median filter is usually better than a box filter because it retains significant image structures (it doesn’t blur detail) and it removes salt & pepper noise almost completely — isolated extreme pixels never become the median. Averaging would smear that 191 into the result.

FilterOperationGaussian noiseSalt & pepperDetail / edges
Box (averaging)Mean of windowGoodPoor (smears)Blurs detail
GaussianWeighted meanGoodPoorFewer edge artefacts, less ringing
Adaptive boxMean if |s−g|<TGoodModeratePreserves edges
MedianMiddle of sorted windowGoodExcellentKeeps edges & structure
11

Self-test quiz

The convolution / noise / edges quiz from the practice set. Tap a question to reveal the answer.

Convolution · Noise · Edge detection
Q1The purpose of the convolution operator is…
  • To detect edges
  • To assess the similarity between an image patch and a template
  • To smooth an image
  • To find objects
Edge detection, smoothing and finding objects are things you can achieve with convolution — but the operator itself assesses similarity between a patch and a template.
Q2Noise in an image is…
  • any factor in the imaging system that gives rise to perturbations in the image
  • random blockiness in the image
  • random fluctuations of the data
  • speckle in the image
Blockiness, fluctuations and speckle are examples; the definition is any factor in the imaging system causing perturbations.
Q3Noise is generally assumed to be…
  • Zero mean, triangular distributed
  • Zero mean, Gaussian distributed
  • Zero mean, uniformly distributed
  • Non-zero mean, Gaussian distributed
Q4The effect of noise can be reduced by…
  • Averaging pixel values over a small locality
  • Choosing a random pixel value in a locality
  • Choosing the modal pixel value in a locality
  • Choosing the smallest pixel value in a locality
Q5One problem with smoothing an image to reduce noise is…
  • It can give incorrect answers
  • It can be expensive to execute
  • It can blur image detail
  • It fails to process pixels at the image edges
Q6Median smoothing is better than averaging because…
  • It is more efficient
  • It retains significant image structures
  • It reduces the effects of noise more
  • It blurs the image less
Q7Gaussian smoothing is a good choice for noise reduction because…
  • It is conceptually simpler
  • It is more efficient to implement
  • It introduces fewer artefacts at the edges of objects
  • It gives improved smoothing results
Q8A square convolution template can be separated into two 1-D templates if…
  • The convolution of the 1-D templates equals the original square template
  • The sum of the 1-D templates equals the original square template
  • The cross product of the 1-D templates equals the original square template
  • The product of the 1-D templates equals the original square template
Q9Convolution of an image with r rows, c columns and an x×y template is…
  • O(rcxy)
  • O(1)
  • O(rc)
  • O(xy)
Every one of the r·c pixels needs x·y multiply-adds.
Q10Template elements are normalised to sum to 1 because…
  • This is computationally more efficient
  • This helps to keep the answers small
  • This is computationally the correct thing to do
  • This helps to ensure the answer does not overflow
Q11An edge in image processing is defined as…
  • The line joining two vertices of a polygon
  • The boundary of the image
  • A significant, local, extended change in image intensity
  • A spatially organised change in image intensity
‘Edge’ means something different in image processing than in computer graphics.
Q12A sensible starting point for locating edges is…
  • To find the direction of the largest increase in grey value at each pixel
  • To subtract values of adjacent pixels along the x and y directions
  • To locate pixels that are local maxima
  • To find the largest difference between a pixel and its eight neighbours
Q13Edges can be enhanced by convolution with…
  • A Mexican-hat template
  • A pair of templates that achieve the subtraction along the x and y directions
  • A template that enhances local maxima
  • A smoothing template followed by a differentiation template
The Mexican-hat does double differentiation — edges are flagged by zero-crossings.
Q14What is the benefit of a 3×3 Sobel template over smaller templates?
  • It is more efficient than smaller templates
  • It computes the differential more accurately
  • It incorporates a small amount of local averaging that suppresses noise
  • It blurs the result less than a smaller template
Q15An edge is a vector. How is its magnitude found?
  • Maximum of the absolute x and y derivatives
  • Adding the absolute values of the x and y derivatives
  • Adding the x and y derivatives in quadrature
  • Sum of the x and y derivatives
‘In quadrature’ = √(δx² + δy²), the Euclidean magnitude.
Q16How is an edge’s direction found?
  • Ratio of the x and y derivatives
  • Maximum of the x and y derivatives
  • Inverse tan of the x and y derivatives
  • Inverse tan of the ratio of the y and x derivatives
Q17Canny derived his edge detector using which three requirements?
  • Edges located, orientation accurate, magnitude accurate
  • Edges located, edges must enclose a region
  • Edges located, extent of the edge identified
  • Edges detected, localisation accurate, single response to an edge
Q18Template matching can find known objects. How do you build a template?
  • The template is a model of the object to be found
  • The template is a binary version of the object
  • The template abstracts the shape of the object
  • The template matches the size of the object
Q19Template matching is a bad choice for object recognition if…
  • The object varies in orientation
  • The object varies in colour
  • The object is quite big
  • There is any variability in the object to be found
Q20Cross-correlation is the operation that…
  • computes the similarity between an image patch and a template
  • corrects colour bias in the image
  • makes rows and columns independent
  • measures the resemblance between a template and the image
12

Past-paper questions

Real COMP27112 exam questions touching this chapter. The core lecture covers simple edges, convolution and noise; some questions extend into Sobel/Prewitt, Laplacian/Marr-Hildreth and Canny — closely related edge-detection material worth knowing.

2022 · Q314 marks

Explain how convolution of an image with a 3×3 kernel is performed. Write down an example of a 3×3 kernel that performs blurring and one which finds edges.

▸ Outline answer / pointers

Describe sliding the kernel over every pixel, multiplying each of the 9 weights by the pixel beneath it, summing, and writing the result to the pixel under the kernel centre (Section 03). State that it cannot be done in-place and needs edge handling.

Blurring (box): all weights 1/9 (or 1s with ÷9 normalisation). Edge-finding (Laplacian): [0 −1 0; −1 4 −1; 0 −1 0] — weights sum to 0 so flat areas give 0 and only intensity changes respond.

2019 · Q274 marks

A box filter, a weighted average filter or a median filter can be used to reduce noise. Define these filters. Comment on their performance.

▸ Outline answer / pointers

Box: mean of the window (all weights 1/N) — reduces Gaussian noise, blurs detail, poor on salt & pepper. Weighted average (e.g. Gaussian): central pixels weighted more, fewer edge artefacts and less ringing. Median (rank filter): sort the window, take the middle value — excellent on salt & pepper, preserves edges/structure, keeps detail. See the comparison table in Section 10.

20173 marks

Images are often corrupted by noise. What is noise? How would you measure the amplitude of the noise? How would you reduce the effect of noise in an image?

▸ Outline answer / pointers

What: deviation of a value from its expected value; any factor in the imaging system causing perturbations; assumed zero-mean Gaussian. Measure amplitude: standard deviation of pixel values over a region that should be uniform. Reduce: spatial smoothing (box/Gaussian/median), adaptive smoothing, or temporal averaging/stacking of multiple frames (Sections 08–10).

20173 marks

Define an edge as it relates to image processing. Use this definition to derive an operator that will highlight edges.

▸ Outline answer / pointers

Edge: a significant, local, extended change in image intensity. Derive operator: differencing adjacent pixels approximates the intensity gradient — |I(x,y) − I(x+1,y)| for vertical edges, |I(x,y) − I(x,y+1)| for horizontal — then combine (OR / city-block / Euclidean magnitude) and threshold (Sections 01–02).

2016 · Q32+2+2+4+8+2

(a) Define an edge. (b) Why are edges important? (c) What is meant by scale in detecting edges? (d) Describe an algorithm that detects edges taking scale into account. (e) Design an algorithm that detects box-like objects using edge detection. (f) Give an account of the effect of image noise on edge detection.

▸ Outline answer / pointers

(a) Significant local extended intensity change. (b) Edges mark object boundaries / features, are compact and largely illumination-invariant, useful for recognition. (c) Scale = the size/extent over which the intensity change occurs; small kernels catch fine edges, larger ones catch coarse edges. (d) Smooth with progressively larger Gaussians (a scale space) and detect edges at each scale. (e) Edge-detect → combine H+V gradients → threshold → look for closed rectangular arrangements of straight edges / corners. (f) Noise creates spurious gradients and breaks real edges; differencing amplifies noise, so smooth (Gaussian/median) first and threshold to suppress weak (noisy) responses.

2018 · Q122 marks

Define an edge. Give one or two template-based techniques for detecting edges in the presence of noise.

▸ Outline answer / pointers

Edge = significant local extended intensity change. Template-based, noise-tolerant detectors: Sobel / Prewitt 3×3 operators — they combine differencing with a little local averaging, which suppresses noise. (The Laplacian-of-Gaussian / Marr-Hildreth smooths first, then differentiates.)

2024 · Essayextension

The Prewitt and Sobel operators are commonly used to find edges. (i) Explain why they also have a slight smoothing effect. (ii) Why do edges around a white square come out two pixels wide? (iii) The kernels come in pairs (one horizontal, one vertical) — what is done with the two intermediate outputs?

▸ Outline answer / pointers

(i) Each operator is a 3×3 that differences across the centre but averages along the perpendicular direction (the 1-2-1 / 1-1-1 column), so it includes local averaging → mild smoothing → noise suppression (this is the quiz fact about 3×3 vs smaller templates). (ii) The difference operator responds on both sides of the intensity step (one response entering the square, one leaving), so each side of the square produces an edge a couple of pixels wide. (iii) Combine the horizontal and vertical responses into one edge image — typically the gradient magnitude √(δx²+δy²) (or |δx|+|δy|) then threshold. Example Sobel pair: [-1 0 1; -2 0 2; -1 0 1] and [-1 -2 -1; 0 0 0; 1 2 1].

2025 · Essayextension

Canny edge detector: (i) What three requirements did Canny set? (ii) First main step? (iii) The basic edge operator produces two images — what are they? (iv) How are orientations quantised? (v) What is non-maximum suppression? (vi) How are the two threshold images combined (hysteresis)?

▸ Outline answer / pointers

(i) Good detection, accurate localisation, and a single response to each edge. (ii) Gaussian smoothing to reduce noise. (iii) A magnitude image and an orientation image. (iv) Orientations are quantised to a few directions (e.g. 0/45/90/135°), not full resolution. (v) Non-maximum suppression thins edges by keeping a magnitude pixel only if it is a local maximum along the gradient direction. (vi) Hysteresis thresholding: strong-edge pixels (above high T) are kept; weak-edge pixels (above low T) are kept only if connected to a strong edge — joining them gives the final edge image.

13

One-page cheat sheet

ConceptThe one thing to remember
Region vs point processingOutput pixel computed from a neighbourhood, not a single pixel.
Vertical edges|I(x,y) − I(x+1,y)| (difference with right neighbour).
Horizontal edges|I(x,y) − I(x,y+1)| (difference with below neighbour).
Combine edgesOR binary images · city-block |δh|+|δv| · Euclidean √(δh²+δv²); then threshold.
Threshold ↑Removes more weak edges & noise (and some real faint edges).
ConvolutionSlide kernel → multiply → sum → write under centre. O(rcxy).
Convolution vs correlationConvolution flips the kernel; correlation doesn’t. Software usually does correlation but says “convolution”. Same for symmetric kernels.
In-place?No for convolution (would corrupt later reads); yes for point processing.
Kernel shapeSquare & odd (3×3, 5×5, 7×7) → well-defined centre.
NormalisationMake weights sum to 1 (e.g. 1/9 box) so brightness is preserved and the result doesn’t overflow a byte.
AssociativeA⊗(B⊗C) = (A⊗B)⊗C — pre-combine kernels.
SeparableSquare = row ⊗ column. 9MN → 6MN for 3×3; 49MN → 14MN for 7×7.
Box filterAll weights 1/N → averaging/blur (soft focus); bigger = blurrier.
Sharpening[0 −1 0; −1 5 −1; 0 −1 0] sums to 1; enhances edges, amplifies noise.
Laplacian[0 −1 0; −1 4 −1; 0 −1 0] sums to 0; zero on flat areas, responds at edges.
NoiseDeviation from expected value; assume zero-mean Gaussian; salt & pepper = random 0/255.
Noise reductionΣ(x+n) ≈ Σ(x); smoothing (loses detail, ringing), adaptive smoothing, temporal stacking.
Gaussian smoothingWeighted by normal distribution → fewer edge artefacts, reduces ringing.
Median (rank) filterSort window, take middle value → kills salt & pepper, keeps edges (not convolution).

End of Chapter 5 visual notes
